did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper utilized a Deep Neural Network (DNN) model constructed using Tensorflow to differentiate between Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset. The model achieved an AUC of 0.912, precision of 81.8%, and accuracy of 83.3%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The provided text contains multiple distinct abstracts from a conference proceeding (J Am Acad Dermatol, September 2024). Specifically, it includes a retrospective chart review study on Prurigo Nodularis (analyzing 363 patients) and a study on advancing the diagnosis of dermatologic conditions using Deep Neural Networks (DNN) on the ISIC dataset (achieving an AUC of 0.912). These are original experimental and observational studies producing new results, not narrative or systematic reviews.

title: Advancing the Diagnosis of Dermatologic Conditions: Utilizing Deep Neural Networks for Accurate Differentiation between Actinic Keratosis and Squamous Cell Carcinoma
brief_summary: This study aimed to demonstrate the potential of machine learning in dermatology by developing a Deep Neural Network (DNN) to differentiate between Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC). The researchers utilized dermoscopic images from the publicly available International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, comprising 114 AK and 131 SCC cases. A DNN model was constructed using Tensorflow and evaluated using metrics such as AUC, precision, recall, specificity, accuracy, and F1 score after splitting the data into training, validation, and test sets. The model achieved an AUC of 0.912, with a recall rate of 81.8%, precision of 81.8%, specificity of 84.6%, accuracy of 83.3%, and an F1 score of 81.8%. The authors concluded that the DNN model effectively differentiates between AK and SCC, showing potential as a screening tool for communities with limited access to healthcare services.

all_task_types.target: Differentiation between Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) using dermoscopic images.
all_task_types.details: The study utilized a Deep Neural Network (DNN) model constructed using Tensorflow to classify dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset into two discrete diagnostic categories: Actinic Keratosis (114 cases) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (131 cases). The model's performance was evaluated using classification metrics including AUC (0.912), precision (81.8%), recall (81.8%), specificity (84.6%), accuracy (83.3%), and F1 score (81.8%).

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available image collection containing dermoscopic images used for the Deep Neural Network (DNN) study to differentiate between Actinic Keratosis and Squamous Cell Carcinoma.

use.training: Images were randomly divided into training (80%), validation (10%), and test sets (10%) for constructing and evaluating a Deep Neural Network (DNN) model.
use.validation: Images were randomly divided into training (80%), validation (10%), and test sets (10%) for constructing and evaluating a Deep Neural Network (DNN) model.
use.testing: Images were randomly divided into training (80%), validation (10%), and test sets (10%) for constructing and evaluating a Deep Neural Network (DNN) model.

datasets_used.name: ADVANCE PN Retrospective Chart Review Cohort
datasets_used.description: A retrospective chart review dataset comprising anonymized data from adult patients newly diagnosed with Prurigo Nodularis (PN) collected from 42 participating sites in Germany between 01/2012 and 12/2022.

use.other: Used for descriptive analysis of patient demographics, clinical manifestations, comorbidities, and treatment patterns in routine care.

datasets_used.name: Teledermatology Program (TDP) Patient Records
datasets_used.description: Retrospective patient records from a non-profit teledermatology program established on Long Island, NY, in June 2022, providing consultations to Ukrainians and Ukrainian refugees.

use.other: Used to identify prevalent skin conditions in a vulnerable population during armed conflict and assess the utility of teledermatology.

datasets_used.name: Keloid and Hypertrophic Scarring Virtual Outreach Event Survey Data
datasets_used.description: Survey data collected from participants of a virtual presentation and interdisciplinary question-and-answer panel organized by the Skin of Color Community (SOCC) regarding keloids and hypertrophic scarring.

use.other: Used to evaluate the effectiveness of the virtual event by comparing pre- and post-event mean knowledge scores and assessing barriers to care.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare pre- and post-event mean knowledge scores regarding keloids and hypertrophic scarring.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference between pre-event and post-event mean knowledge scores regarding keloids and hypertrophic scarring.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference between pre-event and post-event mean knowledge scores regarding keloids and hypertrophic scarring.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study reported a statistically significant increase in knowledge about the etiologies and treatments for keloids (t(481) = 70.0, p < .001), indicating that the virtual outreach event effectively increased participant knowledge.

ISIC_dataset_AK_SCC_Differentiation.model: Deep Neural Network (DNN) constructed using Tensorflow
ISIC_dataset_AK_SCC_Differentiation.notes: The training process lasted for 2 hours and 19 minutes. The model effectively differentiated between Actinic Keratosis (114 cases) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (131 cases).

architecture_details.description: A Deep Neural Network model specifically designed to distinguish between Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) using dermoscopic images.

training_details.dataset: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset (114 AK cases, 131 SCC cases)

conclusions: The Deep Neural Network (DNN) model effectively differentiated between Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) using dermoscopic images, achieving an AUC of 0.912, precision of 81.8%, recall of 81.8%, specificity of 84.6%, accuracy of 83.3%, and an F1 score of 81.8%.
The DNN model demonstrates the potential of AI as a screening tool in dermatology, particularly for communities with limited access to healthcare services.
The retrospective chart review uncovered Prurigo Nodularis (PN) as an underserved disease with a high unmet need in routine care in Germany.
In the PN study, most patients had mild or moderate disease severity, with nodules being the most frequent manifestation, and topical steroids were the most frequent first-line therapy.
Patient-reported outcomes and clinical scores were rarely used for PN diagnosis, and itch measurements were performed infrequently.

The non-profit teledermatology program (TDP) for Ukrainians and refugees identified seborrheic dermatitis, acne, and rosacea as common diagnoses, with nearly half of the top three diagnoses representing acute exacerbations of pre-existing conditions.
The TDP study highlights the potential of teledermatology to extend care to patients without access to specialist services during armed conflict, despite a limited sample size.
Virtual outreach events on keloids and hypertrophic scarring significantly increased participant knowledge about etiologies and treatments (p < .001).

Post-event data indicated that participants were more likely to visit a dermatologist for keloid treatments (86%), better advocate for themselves (96%), and wear sunscreen to improve scar discoloration (96%).
Barriers to care for keloids and hypertrophic scarring included cost, time constraints, concern for discomfort/pain with treatment, and lack of access to a dermatologist.
important_remarks: The AI/DNN study is a conference abstract with a very small dataset (245 total images: 114 AK, 131 SCC), limiting generalizability.
The DNN model was trained on the publicly available ISIC dataset, which may introduce selection bias compared to real-world clinical populations.
The DNN study lacks external validation on independent datasets; performance metrics (AUC 0.912, Accuracy 83.3%) are based on a single internal split (80/10/10).
The Prurigo Nodularis (PN) chart review is company-sponsored by Sanofi, which may introduce sponsorship bias regarding the characterization of 'unmet need'.
The PN study excluded specialized pruritus centers, potentially underestimating disease severity and treatment complexity in routine care settings.
The Teledermatology study for Ukrainian refugees has a very small sample size (n=26), limiting statistical power and representativeness.
The virtual outreach event for keloids/hypertrophic scarring relied on self-reported survey data with a 65% response rate, introducing potential self-selection bias.
The PN study notes that patient-reported outcomes and clinical scores were rarely used in routine care (8.8% and 3.3% respectively), highlighting a gap in standardized assessment.
The DNN training duration (2 hours 19 minutes) is reported but key hyperparameters (learning rate, optimizer, layers) are not detailed in the abstract.

The paper aggregates multiple distinct studies (AI, PN chart review, Teledermatology, Virtual Outreach) in one abstract, making it difficult to assess the specific methodological rigor of each component.
